Empirical labor-supply substitution and primary-earner semi-elasticity magnitudes from U.S. microsimulation practice
Elasticity calibration ranges in common tax-benefit modeling (bounded wage-change response conventions)
runs.reasoningSummary: I place the decile-7 primary-earner substitution elasticity slightly below unity, reflecting moderate responsiveness of labor supply/earnings to changes in net-of-tax incentives for middle-to-upper deciles, with substantial but not extreme uncertainty (narrower than very low-income ranges, wider than the most stable near-median estimates).

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 7 in a U.S. microsimulation model.
citations: Recall anchor: behavioral elasticity ranges commonly used in U.S. tax-microsimulation (e.g., labor supply/response parameters at the intensive-margin)
Recall anchor: evidence that substitution/participation responses vary across earnings deciles, typically with moderate elasticities in middle-to-upper middle deciles
Recall anchor: modeling practice in microsimulations that bounds effective marginal wage changes and uses elasticity-like response coefficients
runs.reasoningSummary: I place the decile-7 primary-earner substitution elasticity at a moderately sized, positive value, with uncertainty spanning roughly half to low-high unit magnitudes; the median reflects a middle-of-the-road response given typical calibration patterns and decile heterogeneity.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 7 in a U.S. microsimulation model.
citations: Standard microeconometric practice of expressing behavioral responses as elasticities in tax-benefit simulations
Typical labor-supply substitution elasticities used in structural/partial-equilibrium microsimulation (small-to-moderate magnitudes)
Empirical findings in labor supply literature often showing heterogeneous elasticities by income group, with modest responses in mid-income deciles
runs.reasoningSummary: I place the median in a modest positive range, reflecting that primary earners in mid-income deciles often show limited but non-negligible responsiveness of labor-related outcomes to effective marginal wage changes, with substantial uncertainty and heterogeneity (hence wide tails).

runs.reasoningSummary: I expect moderate substitution elasticities for primary earners around middle-income deciles in U.S. tax-benefit simulations, with uncertainty spanning from relatively low responsiveness to somewhat higher values under variation in incentives, job/earnings margins, and interaction with means-tested programs.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 7 in a U.S. microsimulation model.
citations: Eissa-Grogger-MacDougall labor supply elasticity framework and tax incidence intuition
Moffitt/Keane labor supply substitution and elasticities by income group (qualitative recollection)

runs.reasoningSummary: I currently expect the decile-7 primary-earner substitution response to be roughly on the order of 1 (elasticity ≈ 1), with moderate uncertainty: possibly as low as ~0.5 and plausibly up to ~2.0 in more responsive scenarios.
